food_name: Hot Dog (Beef Frankfurter in Bun with Ketchup and Mustard)
aliases: hot dog with ketchup and mustard

english: hot dog with ketchup and mustard

cuisine: American / Singapore food court and cinema
region_of_origin: United States (American origin); widely available at Singapore food courts, cinema stalls, and shopping mall kiosks
food_subgroup: Processed beef frankfurter (~50g, pre-cooked, steamed or grilled) served in a soft white hot dog bun with ketchup and yellow mustard

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has frankfurter as a concept (FOODON_03301050). The Singapore hot dog as a complete preparation — beef frank in white bun with ketchup and mustard — is the applied dish form; no exact FoodOn URI exists for the assembled product.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hot dog — beef frankfurter in white bun with ketchup and mustard
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00MK is the closest parent for hot dog / frankfurter preparations. The Singapore food-court variant with condiments is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to beef frankfurter in bun with condiments as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 hot dog with bun and condiments
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hot dog serving is approximately 140g, comprising a ~50g pre-cooked beef frankfurter in a soft white hot dog bun (~75g) dressed with ketchup and yellow mustard (~15g combined), as commonly served at Singapore food courts and cinema kiosks.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Dog — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Frankfurter, beef) and USDA SR Legacy (Hot dog bun, white bread roll) composite
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy data for beef frankfurter and white hot dog bun, weighted to reflect the Singapore food-court standard assembly (~50g frank, ~75g bun, ~15g condiments). Ketchup and mustard contributions to sodium and sugar are incorporated. Values represent the composite per-100g profile of the assembled product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Bread-Based Foods and Frankfurter Preparations — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~70 (high) is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for hot dog / white bun preparations. The primary glycaemic driver is the soft white hot dog bun (GI ~70–75 for white bread rolls); the beef frankfurter itself contributes negligible glycaemic load. GL per 140g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 34.3) / 100 = 24.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 748mg per 100g; a single 140g serving delivers approximately 1,047mg sodium, approaching half the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should significantly limit or avoid hot dogs; the beef frankfurter is the primary sodium source (curing salts: sodium chloride and sodium nitrite), and ketchup adds further sodium — requesting the hot dog without ketchup and mustard reduces sodium by approximately 100–150mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~70) from the soft white hot dog bun and ketchup sugar (7.5g sugar/100g); glycaemic load per 140g serving is 24.0, which is in the high GL range
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume hot dogs only occasionally; replacing the white bun with a wholegrain or lower-GI alternative and omitting ketchup would substantially reduce the glycaemic load, while the high sodium and processed meat content remain independent metabolic concerns.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined adverse cardiovascular profile: trans fat (0.5g/100g), saturated fat (5.8g/100g), very high sodium (748mg/100g), cholesterol (48mg/100g), and ultra-processed meat classification (WHO Group 2A carcinogen for processed meat)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of beef frankfurters; the trans fat and saturated fat content raise LDL cholesterol, the high sodium elevates blood pressure, and processed meat intake is independently associated with increased cardiovascular mortality in prospective cohort studies — this food should be treated as an occasional indulgence only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.5g/100g), sodium nitrite-derived nitrosamines (proinflammatory), and ultra-processed meat classification associated with elevated CRP and IL-6 inflammatory markers
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid processed beef frankfurters; sodium nitrite converts to nitrosamines in the stomach (classified as probable carcinogens and significantly proinflammatory), and multiple cohort studies associate processed meat consumption with increased systemic inflammation — the modest zinc and B12 content do not offset these risks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The hot dog is a ubiquitous fast food item available at Singapore food courts, cinema kiosks, and shopping mall food stalls. It consists of a pre-cooked beef frankfurter — a blended, emulsified, cured beef sausage seasoned with spices, garlic, and curing salts (sodium nitrite and sodium chloride) — nestled in a soft white hot dog bun and dressed with ketchup and yellow mustard. In Singapore, beef frankfurters are the standard offering at halal-certified stalls, though pork variants exist at non-halal establishments. The frankfurter is typically steamed or grilled before serving. Texturally, the frank is smooth, slightly springy, and juicy with a mild smoky-savoury flavour; the white bun is soft and slightly sweet. Nutritionally, the hot dog is characterised by very high sodium (748mg/100g; ~1,047mg per 140g serving), notable trans fat (0.5g/100g from emulsified processed fat in the frankfurter), saturated fat (5.8g/100g), and a high glycaemic index (~70) driven by the white bun and ketchup. The glycaemic load per standard serving is 24.0 (high). Clinically, the hot dog is a high-concern food for hypertension (sodium), cardiovascular disease (trans fat, saturated fat, sodium, processed meat), and rheumatoid arthritis (nitrosamines from sodium nitrite, proinflammatory processed meat). It is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4). The beef frankfurter is a WHO Group 2A probable carcinogen category food (processed meat). Despite these concerns, the hot dog provides moderate protein (11.5g/100g), useful B12 (1.0mcg/100g), zinc (2.0mg/100g), and niacin (3.5mg/100g) from the beef content.
common_user_queries: beef hot dog Singapore food court

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef frankfurter and white hot dog bun components, which are well-documented staple foods in the USDA database. The composite per-100g profile for the assembled Singapore food-court hot dog is adapted from these approved primary sources with documented weighting. GI referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white bun-based hot dog preparations. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology anchors are defensible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite assembled hot dog (frank + bun + condiments) is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy at the composite level; individual component data exists but composite MUFA/PUFA split is not available as a single verified figure. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite assembled product is not reported as a single figure in USDA SR Legacy. All other per_100g fields are populated with verified numeric values. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white bread roll-based hot dog preparations; the white bun is the primary glycaemic driver. Halal status applies to beef frankfurter variants at certified stalls; pork variants exist at non-halal establishments. Trans fat value of 0.5g/100g reflects processed fat content in the beef frankfurter emulsion as reported in USDA SR Legacy for beef frankfurter products.
